problem text: Determine the derivative of $f(x) = (\ln(x))^x$ at the point $x = e$.
problem solution: Utilize logarithmic differentiation for this problem. Define $y = f(x) = (\ln(x))^x$. Then, $\ln(y) = x\ln(\ln(x))$. By applying the product and chain rules, we find $\dfrac{y'}{y} = \ln(\ln(x)) + x \cdot \dfrac{1}{x\ln(x)} \iff y' = y\left[\ln(\ln(x)) + \dfrac{1}{\ln(x)}\right] = (\ln(x))^x\left[\ln(\ln(x)) + \dfrac{1}{\ln(x)}\right]$. Substituting $x = e$ into this expression results in a value of $1$.
problem hint: Consider using logarithmic differentiation for this problem.
